coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for “off-on” sensing of Hg(II) and I −
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Jiangling
Zhang, Haoran
Zou, Jinliang
Liu, Yingliang
Zhuang, Jianle
Xiao, Yong
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a simple, one-step reflux method for synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) using citric acid as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine (DETA) as the surface passivation reagent along with a high quantum yield (82.40%), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was found to be effectively quenched by Hg(II) ions. Upon addition of I− to the CDs/Hg(II) complex dispersion, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was significantly recovered. Furthermore, we developed an “off-on” fluorescence assay for the detection of I− using CDs/Hg(II) as a fluorescence probe. This probe enables the selective detection of Hg(II) with a linear range of 0–80μM and a limit of detection is 0.201µM and a limit of detection about I− is 0.234µM with a linear range of 0–70μM. Most importantly, the sensors can be successfully applied to the determination of Hg(II) and I− in real lake water and urine of cattles, the “off-on” sensor demonstrates high selectivity, repeatability, stability, which offer this CDs-based “off-on” fluorescent sensor a promising platform for environmental and biological sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Fluorescence
Off-On
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs, and the schematic of the fabrication and sensing process of Hg(II) and I−
CDs-based fluorescent chemosensor for sensitive and selective detection of Hg(II)
Selectivity of the chemosensor for Hg(II) detection
Sensitivity of the chemosensor for Hg(II) detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant nos. 51203053 , 21571067 , 51372091 ), the Foundation for High-level Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong Province, the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province, the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant no. S2013030012842 ), and the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province ( 2012B010200030 , 2014A010105035 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Normalized fluorescence intensity (λ ex=360nm) of aqueous CDs solution (0.01mg/mL) in the presence of 25μM of various metal ions. (B) Selective fluorescence intensity response of aqueous CDs solution towards 25μM Hg(II) (blue bars), and interference of 25μM of other metal ions with 25μM Hg(II) (red bars). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs in aqueous solution upon addition of various concentrations of Hg(II) (from top to bottom: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75 and 80μM, λ ex=360nm). (D) Plot of the fluorescence intensity against Hg(II) concentration within the range of 0–80μM. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. Inset: linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and Hg(II) concentration. I0 represents the fluorescence intensity without Hg(II), and I represents the fluorescence intensity with different Hg(II) concentration (λ ex=360nm; λ em=440nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence response of the CDs dispersion in the presence of Hg(II) ions (50μM) to various substances. The concentrations of these substances were 25μM. (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs, CDs/Hg(II) and CDs/Hg(II)/I− mixture in aqueous solution. (C) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of Hg(II) ions (50μM) upon addition of I− with different concentrations within 0–70μM. (D) The fluorescence recovery factor (I−I0)/I0 versus I− concentration within 0 to 70μM. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. Inset: linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and I− concentration. I0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of 50μM Hg(II), and I is the recovered fluorescence intensity after the addition of different I− concentration (λ ex=360nm; λ em=440nm).
Schematic of the fabrication and sensing process of Hg(II) and I−.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for “off-on” sensing of Hg(II) and I −

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a simple, one-step reflux method for synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) using citric acid as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine (DETA) as the surface passivation reagent along with a high quantum yield (82.40%), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was found to be effectively quenched by Hg(II) ions. Upon addition of I− to the CDs/Hg(II) complex dispersion, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was significantly recovered. Furthermore, we developed an “off-on” fluorescence assay for the detection of I− using CDs/Hg(II) as a fluorescence probe. This probe enables the selective detection of Hg(II) with a linear range of 0–80μM and a limit of detection is 0.201µM and a limit of detection about I− is 0.234µM with a linear range of 0–70μM. Most importantly, the sensors can be successfully applied to the determination of Hg(II) and I− in real lake water and urine of cattles, the “off-on” sensor demonstrates high selectivity, repeatability, stability, which offer this CDs-based “off-on” fluorescent sensor a promising platform for environmental and biological sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of luminescent nanomaterials with exciting properties that make them applied extensively in massive fields. CDs are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with sizes under 10nm, which are firstly discovered during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004). Owing to their excellent and unique properties, carbon dots have recently drawn a lot of attention. Compared to traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (Mishra et al., 2009; Gao et al., 2004), photoluminescent CDs are superior in terms of high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization, high resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity and good biocompatibility (Baker, S.N. and Baker, G.A. 2010; Hu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2013; Bourlinos et al., 2008). As a result, it makes them promising candidates for numerous exciting applications, such as bioimaging (Li et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011), photocatalysis (Li et al., 2010), LED (Guo et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2010), energy conversion (Chandra et al., 2013), photovoltaic devices (Gupta et al., 2011; Feng et al., 2009; Yan et al.,2010), medical diagnosis (Kong et al., 2012) and sensing (Zhu et al., 2013; Qu et al., 2013). Now, many approaches for the synthesis of CDs have been reported, such as combustion, ultrasonic treatment, arc discharge, electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, thermal treatment, acid reflux and microwave methods. However, the synthesis process of these methods is relatively complex, post-processing is cumbersome, the need of high temperature or hazardous reagents such as strong acid, alkali etc. Therefore, it is essential to develop a simple, convenient and low-cost method for the synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots along with a high quantum yield, good photophysical and low toxicity.

Ion pollution has become a critical worldwide issue due to the severe risks in organisms and environment. Currently, many CDs solution has been used to detect ions in aqueous solutions. Fluorescent carbon dots prepared from EDTA have also been used to assay mercury ions Hg(II) in aqueous solution (Zhou et al., 2012). Wee et al. prepared CDs from bovine serum albumin (Wee et al., 2013). The fluorescence of the CDs can be significantly quenched particularly by Pb2+ in a selective manner. Liu et al. proposed a low cost, green method to synthesize fluorescent polymer nanodots from grass, and the fluorescent nanodots could detect Cu2+, but there is a pity that iron ions (Fe3+) at high concentration can interfere in the detection of Cu2+ ions (Liu et al., 2012). CDs prepared from citric acid under the passivation of poly (ethylenimine) could be used for sensitively detection of Cu2+ with a detection limit of 6nM (Dong et al., 2012). Jin's group synthesized fluorescent graphene oxide sheets after the oxidation of graphene oxide with periodic acid, and the fluorescent graphene oxide sheets could be used as a detection sensor of Fe3+ (Wang et al., 2012). Although many kinds of CDs have been developed as fluorescent probes for detection ions, it is still expecting to develop bran-new methods for the preparation of more stable and highly fluorescent carbon dots, and making them widely applied.

In our work, we have developed a facile, convenient and low-cost approach to synthesize graphene carbon dots (CDs) with blue emission through reflux treatment of citric acid in the presence of diethylenetriamine (DETA) at 200°C for 5h. Compared with the previous work, this method does not need expensive reagents, strong acid, strong base or further surface passivation, which enables the possibility of synthesis in large scale. The as-prepared CDs have a narrow size distribution around 5–7nm and exhibit excitation-independent properties along with a high quantum yield of about 82.40%. Remarkably, the as-prepared CDs show good stability in high-salt conditions and can be used to detect Hg(II) and I− in an aqueous solution, which is the first report to detect them at the same time, indicating their potential applications in environmental and biomedical fields. Significant fluorescence (FL) quenching of the CDs occurs upon addition of different concentrations of Hg(II) ions. And FL also recovered after the addition of I−. These CDs can serve as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Hg(II) ions and I− with high sensitivity and selectivity. The CDs can also be successfully applied to the determination of Hg(II) and I− in real lake water and urine samples.
Including of materials; characterization; preparation of CDs; Quantum yield (QY) measurement; Fluorescent probe for the detections of Hg(II) ion and I− are shown in the Supplimentary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs, and the schematic of the fabrication and sensing process of Hg(II) and I−
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were prepared via the reflux treatment of citric acid with DETA at 200°C for 5h, yielding a brown yellow dispersion. Optional preparation conditions are shown in supplementary information (Table S1, Table S2, Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). The prepared CDs were high stability under UV light and day-light (Fig. S8) and their detailed characterization are shown in Fig. S3, Fig. S4 and Fig. S5. In this system, citric acid was chosen as the carbon source and DETA was used as the surface passivation reagent. Scheme 1 directly expresses the fabrication of CDs and the “off-on” quenching process, the fluorescence of CDs solution was seriously quenched with adding Hg(II), but obviously recovered after the addition of I−. Fluorescence probes can form twisted intramolecular charge-transfer states, and the mechanism of photo-induced electron transfer has been used to develop sensors for metal transfer, these sensors often rely on the well known quenching by amines due to PET (Lakowicz, 2013). Photo-induced electron transfer (PET) can occur in lumophore systems (Prasanna de Silva et al., 1997). So based on these inferences, we proposed this principle. The fluorescence of C-Dots arises from the radiative recombination under excitations, which is similar to the QDs (Xia and Zhu, 2008). On the other hand, Hg2+ can quench the fluorescence of QDs due to facilitating non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation through an effective electron transfer process (Liu et al., 2009). Our strategy was inspired by these phenomena and the approach is depicted as below: the fluorescence of CDs solution was seriously quenched with adding Hg(II), but obviously recovered after the addition of I−. Excited state CDs return to the ground state under the influence of UV-light, emitting bright blue emission. We predict that the quenching phenomenon arose from electron transfer, with the addition of Hg(II) ions to the CDs aqueous solution, Hg(II) combines with -OH and -COOH on the surface of CDs, resulting that the electron transfers from excited state of CDs to empty “D” orbit of Hg(II), which leads to the fluorescence quenching of CDs. After the addition of I− ions to the system of CDs/Hg(II), the mercury(II) ion was extracted from the complex due to the stronger binding between mercury(II) ions and iodide, leading to the release of the free dyad and restoration of the fluorescence, and namely the stronger acting force between I− and Hg(II), and leading to the formation of [HgI4]2− so that the fluorescence recovered (Ma et al., 2011). To confirm this speculation, the structural and optical characterization of the synthesized CDs and their fluorescence response to Hg(II) were investigated in this study ( seen from the supplementary information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-based fluorescent chemosensor for sensitive and selective detection of Hg(II)
Selectivity of the chemosensor for Hg(II) detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the present sensing system, two comparative experiments were performed. First, as shown in Fig. 1A, the fluorescence response of CDs was studied in the presence of different metal ions at the concentration of 25μM. It was noticeable that only Hg(II) could induce a serious decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs, implying that CDs could be used to selectively detect Hg(II) in an aqueous solution. In the presence of 25μM of various metal ions (Li+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ag+ and Mn2+), significant fluorescence quenching effect was observed with the addition of Hg(II), while the other metal ions showed only a slight quenching effect and the influence was almost negligible. The high selectivity of CDs to Hg(II) may be attributed to the fact that the interaction between Hg(II) and the carboxylate or hydroxyl groups made CDs close to each other, which accelerated the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through an effective electron transfer process, leading to a substantial decrease of the fluorescence of CDs (Zhou et al., 2012). In another contrast experiment, 25μM of Hg(II) alone (blue bars, Fig. 1B) and the mixtures of 25μM of Hg(II) and 25μM of the above-mentioned metal ions (red bars, Fig. 1B) were added into the CDs aqueous solution, respectively, then the quenching effects of Hg(II) and the mixtures of Hg(II)+Mn+ on the fluorescence of CDs were examined. From the results shown in Fig. 1B, in comparison with the efficient quenching effect of Hg(II), the influence from the other coexisting metal ions was negligible. These observations indicate that the CDs sensor is insensitive to other metal ions, but selective to Hg(II) in the mixtures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the chemosensor for Hg(II) detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on above fluorescent properties of CDs, different concentrations of Hg(II) solution were added to the aqueous solution of CDs and the fluorescence emission intensity was measured to study the sensitivity of the CDs chemosensor. As shown in Fig. 1C, the FL intensity of the CDs at around 440nm decreased gradually with Hg(II) concentration increasing, indicating that addition of Hg(II) ions could effectively quench the fluorescence of the CDs. Fig. 1D presents the fluorescence intensity under various concentrations of Hg(II) ions, the fluorescence signal of CDs gradually faded as the concentration of Hg(II) varied from 0 to 80μM. A good linear correlation over the range from 0 to 30μM with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9982 was obtained (Fig. 1D inset). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.201μM based on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD=3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of y-intercept of the regression line and S is the slope of the calibration curve). It should be noted that the obtained detection limit of the CDs sensor of Hg(II) was much lower than those previously reported comparing with other different fluorescent probes (Table S3). The results show that our sensing system exhibits some superior sensitivity to the previously reported sensing system and the sensor based on CDs may be useful in environmental applications for mercury detection.

The recovery properties of the CDs after Hg(II) detection have also been investigated by adding I− into the CDs/Hg(II) solution. As observed above, the addition of Hg(II) ions can quench the fluorescence of the CDs. However, as shown in Fig. 2A, the quenched fluorescence intensity can be recovered immediately upon the addition of I− into the system of CDs/Hg(II), whereas no obvious signal change was observed for other substances, so the high selectivity of this probe provides a potential for the efficient detection of I−. Fig. 2B obviously shows the FL recovery of CDs/Hg(II) system after the addition of I−. As shown in Fig. 2C, the FL intensity gradually recovered, and even restored to its original intensity with the increasing concentration of I−, such result indicates that the CDs/Hg(II) based sensing system is sensitive to I−, and the “off” state of CDs quenched by Hg(II) can be turned “on” by I−. In Fig. 2D, the value of (I−I0)/I0 increased gradually with increasing I− concentration and a linear calibration in the range from 0 to 25μM was obtained (R 2=0.9987), the limit of detection was estimated to be 0.234μM based on three times signal-to-noise ration. Such detection method is comparable or superior to other methods for the detection of I− (Table S4), because the detection method demonstrated in this system is facile, low-cost and efficient. These advantages offer this CDs-based “off-on” fluorescent probe an excellent platform for the detection of I−. And we have also applied this CDs-based “off-on” fluorescent probe in the real samples (lake water and urine of the cattle). The detection for Hg(II) and I− are shown in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7, respectively, this sensing system can still work for the sensitive detection of Hg(II) and I−. The result indicates that the chemosensor may be a promising sensing platform in real environmental and biologic samples.

In summary, we have developed a facile, convenient and low-cost reflux method for the fabrication of CDs with strong fluorescence quenching response to Hg(II). After the addition of I−, the fluorescence recovered obviously. Based on this fluorescence quenching-recovery phenomenon, we summarized an “off-on” method for the detection of I− using CDs/Hg(II) as a probe for the real samples application. It is believed that the present strategy may offer a new approach for developing low-cost, high sensitive and selective sensors in environmental applications and biological sensing applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant nos. 51203053, 21571067, 51372091), the Foundation for High-level Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong Province, the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province, the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant no. S2013030012842), and the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (2012B010200030, 2014A010105035).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2015.12.084.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) Probes for Metal Ions and Anino Sensors
